classification.title: Kentucky Corporation Laws - 1909, § 581, § 582, § 583
classification.summary: These sections of the Kentucky Corporation Laws from 1909 outline regulations for banks. Section 581 prohibits banks from taking their own capital stock as security for loans and limits the amount of capital stock any single person can own. Section 582 dictates how banks can invest their money, restricting them from engaging in general enterprises but allowing them to hold collateral and acquire real estate for business purposes or in satisfaction of debts. Section 583 sets a limit on the total indebtedness a bank can permit from any single stockholder, person, company, or firm, requiring additional collateral or mortgages for amounts exceeding twenty percent of the bank's paid-in capital stock and surplus.

classification.summary: This act prohibits corporations that own bridges spanning boundary rivers of the Commonwealth from being controlled by or having interlocking ownership with other corporations owning similar bridges. It also forbids contracts, agreements, or arrangements related to tolls or fares between such competing bridge corporations and their officers or stockholders. Furthermore, it prevents individuals or corporations from holding stock in competing bridge companies under specific circumstances. Violations are subject to fines and any contracts made in violation are void.

It focuses on preventing the consolidation of power and control within the bridge industry to ensure fair competition and prevent price-fixing. There is no mention of race, and the regulations apply universally to all corporations and individuals involved in this specific industry, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.
